title: The half hitch (left to right)
subtitle: A single-cord knot that forms diagonal bars and spiral columns
excerpt: The half hitch is one loop of a working cord over a carrier cord. A single half hitch does not lock on its own; the double half hitch (two loops in sequence) is the stable form used in rows and columns. Understanding the half hitch in isolation makes the double half hitch and spiral half-hitch column legible when they appear in patterns.
sourceNotes: The half hitch is a standard knot in rope work, knotting, and macramé. Observation-led.

content.text: is one pass of a working cord around a

. It does not hold as a single knot; two consecutive half hitches in the same direction form the double half hitch, which locks. This entry teaches the mechanics of the single half hitch so the double half hitch and the spiral half-hitch column are fully understood when they appear in pattern instructions.
Pin one cord horizontally as the carrier cord. Hold it firmly at the right end or pin both ends so it stays taut.

steps: Hold the working cord in your left hand. Bring it over the carrier cord from left to right.
Loop the working cord under the carrier cord, moving right to left.
Bring the working cord up and through the loop it has just formed on the left side of itself.
Pull the working cord downward to tighten. One half hitch is now seated on the carrier cord.
Note: the knot does not lock: a slight tug on the working cord will slide it along the carrier. Two consecutive half hitches are needed to hold.

content.text: column is called for in a pattern, the instruction is to repeat the same half hitch direction (left over right, consistently) down a vertical carrier cord. After 4 to 6 repetitions, the column begins to twist naturally. To work a spiral column: hold one cord vertical as the carrier, and knot a working cord around it with single half hitches all in the same direction. The twist becomes visible at around the 5th knot.

Two left-to-right half hitches in a row make a left-to-right double half hitch. It sits firmly. If you swap directions on the same carrier, with one left-to-right then one right-to-left, the twists cancel out. That pairing is rarely used to build the work. It turns up now and then as a small decorative knot between rows of square knots.

attrs.intro: Two common problems when practising the half hitch.

items.fix: Follow with a second half hitch in the same direction to form a double half hitch, which locks. A single half hitch holds only under constant tension from above.
items.cause: A single half hitch is not self-locking. This is correct behaviour.
items.symptom: Knot slides along the carrier when the working cord is pulled

items.fix: Untie back to the last consistent knot and resume in the correct direction. Once all knots run the same way, the spiral begins to show within 4 to 5 knots.
items.cause: Direction has been swapped accidentally: one or more knots were tied right over left instead of left over right.
items.symptom: Spiral column not twisting after 6 knots

attrs.heading: Common problems
